local_names.japanese: スパイシーサーモン丼

aliases: スパイシーサーモン丼
Salmon Rice Bowl with Spice Mix

english: Salmon Rice Bowl with Spice Mix
japanese: スパイシーサーモン丼

food_subgroup: Japanese rice bowl with raw fish (donburi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spicy salmon donburi; closest parent 'salmon dish' (FOODON_00002809) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for spicy salmon donburi; closest parent is raw salmon (A0CKP). The dish includes cooked rice and spice mix components not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for spicy salmon donburi as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 150 g of sashimi-grade salmon, 180 g of steamed Japanese short-grain rice, and 50 g of spice mix, sauce, and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon, raw (Atlantic, farmed) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15236; Japanese short-grain rice, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #20444; composite dish estimated by weighted ingredient assembly
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Spicy Salmon Don exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values estimated by weighted assembly: approximately 39% raw Atlantic salmon (farmed), 47% cooked Japanese short-grain rice, and 14% spice mix / sauce / sesame oil components. Salmon macros from USDA SR Legacy #15236; rice macros from USDA SR Legacy #20444; spice/sauce contribution estimated from USDA condiment entries. Per-100g values reflect the blended composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~54–56; composite dish GI estimated from rice component dominance
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Salmon Don as a composite dish. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 54–56 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The salmon and spice components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and are expected to slightly attenuate the overall GI via protein and fat co-ingestion. GI set to 54 (low, ≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 67.6) / 100 ≈ 36.5, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 17.8 × 3.8 = 67.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning blend
hypertension.guidance: A single 380 g serving contributes approximately 798 mg sodium (~35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), primarily from soy sauce and spice mix; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low GI (~54) but a moderate glycemic load (~36.5 per serving) due to the substantial white rice portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller rice portion or substitute with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, while the high-quality salmon protein and omega-3 fats are beneficial for insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acid content from salmon
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sashimi-grade Atlantic salmon provides significant EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids (~1.5–2 g per serving), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; saturated fat is low (~3.4 g per serving), making this dish broadly cardioprotective when sodium from soy sauce is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Salmon Don (スパイシーサーモン丼) is a Japanese donburi rice bowl dish consisting of sashimi-grade raw Atlantic salmon sliced and arranged over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, dressed with a signature spicy seasoning blend that typically includes chilli, sesame oil, soy sauce, and aromatic spices. The dish is served in a single bowl and is a popular item in Japanese casual dining and Japanese-inspired restaurants across Singapore. The salmon is served raw in the sashimi tradition, offering a silky, buttery texture with a mild oceanic flavour that is complemented by the heat and umami of the spice mix. The rice base provides a soft, slightly sticky foundation. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie meal (~562 kcal per 380 g serving) with a strong protein contribution (~37 g per serving) from the salmon, which is also an excellent source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, selenium, and niacin. The carbohydrate content is moderate, derived almost entirely from the white rice base, giving the dish a low glycemic index (~54) but a moderate glycemic load per serving. Sodium is a notable concern due to soy sauce in the spice mix. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians. It is not suitable for those with fish, soy, sesame, or gluten allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Spicy Salmon Don as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted ingredient assembly using USDA SR Legacy data for raw Atlantic salmon and cooked Japanese short-grain rice, which are well-characterised in approved sources. GI was estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (Japanese white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact spice mix composition and sauce quantities from The Oyster Bank are not publicly documented, introducing uncertainty in sodium and minor nutrient values.
notes: Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy data for the salmon and rice components — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese donburi restaurant portions; the actual portion at The Oyster Bank - Funan may differ. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g (798 mg per serving) is based on a moderate soy sauce contribution and may underestimate actual sodium if the restaurant uses a generous sauce application. The cuisine_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it does not describe this food. Vitamin D is reported as 5.2 mcg per 100 g, consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for raw Atlantic farmed salmon. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite estimation and are not confirmed absent; they reflect data not available at the composite dish level rather than confirmed zero values. Specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all individually estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and reported as positive numeric values above; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record as all fields were estimable from the approved source components.
